24: The note that Sheerah ‘built Lower Beth-horon and Upper Beth-horon and Uzzen-sheerah’ — ûḇittô šᵉʾērāh wayyiḇen ʾet bêt ḥôrôn haṭṭaḥtônāh wᵉʾet hāʿelyônāh wᵉʾet ʾuzzên šᵉʾērāh — is one of the OT’s most striking attributions: a woman founder of towns that are strategically central to Israel’s history. Beth-horon (Upper and Lower) controlled the ascent from the Aijalon Valley in the Shephelah into the Judean hill country — the main western approach to Jerusalem. The site appears repeatedly in Israelite military history: Joshua pursued the Amorite coalition through Beth-horon after the Gibeon battle, and YHWH threw down great stones on the fleeing enemy there (Joshua 10:10-11); it marks the tribal boundary between Benjamin and Ephraim (Joshua 16:3,5; 1 Chronicles 18:13-14); the Philistines raided through the Beth-horon pass (1 Samuel 13:18); and Judas Maccabaeus defeated Seron at Beth-horon in 166 BCE (1 Macc 1 Chronicles 3:16-24). Solomon later fortified both towns (1 Kings 9:17). Archaeological survey of the Beth-horon sites (Beit Ur et-Tahta and Beit Ur el-Foqa) has identified Iron Age remains consistent with their role as defended pass-towns. The Chronicler’s attribution of the towns’ founding to Sheerah (who also gives her name to Uzzen-sheerah, ‘the ear/district of Sheerah’) preserves a tribal tradition of female civic agency that ANE genealogical texts occasionally record for shrines and chapels but rarely for military-strategic fortifications.
1: Issachar’s territory lay in the Jezreel valley and the lower Galilee slopes — the most fertile agricultural land in Canaan. The valley floor (modern Marj Ibn Amir) was renowned in antiquity for its grain production. Issachar’s four sons match the Numbers 26:23–25 census tradition; their position in the tribal genealogy between the northern group and the Joseph tribes reflects their central geographical position between Galilee and Manasseh’s highland territory.
2: The figure of 22,600 warriors in David’s day (from Tola’s line alone) reflects the Chronicler’s use of tribal census data. The Jezreel valley’s agricultural wealth would have supported a dense population: the valley received reliable winter rainfall and was easily worked. Egyptian records from the New Kingdom period list Jezreel settlements as major administrative centres under Pharaonic control, confirming the region’s economic and demographic significance before and during the early Israelite monarchy.
3: Izrahiah’s five sons all designated “leaders” suggests the Issacharite aristocracy of David’s period was organized around multiple clan-heads of equal standing rather than a single paramount chief. This collegial leadership structure is attested in the Amarna letters (14th century BCE), where Canaanite city-states in the Jezreel valley were governed by councils of local leaders alongside Pharaonic representatives.
4: The 36,000-troop figure for Izrahiah’s line, attributed to large households (“many wives and sons”), reflects the ancient Near Eastern connection between polygyny and political power. In pre-monarchic and early monarchic Canaan, having multiple wives was a marker of elite status that also produced the large male offspring needed to sustain military and administrative networks. The genealogy here functions as a militia registration: tribal muster-lists were kept for the same purposes as modern census and military draft records.
5: The 87,000 total Issacharite warriors makes Issachar one of the largest tribal contingents in the Davidic military system. This contrasts with the Numbers 26 census (54,400) and may reflect a different administrative moment or a different counting methodology (by households vs. by individuals). Issachar’s military prominence is consistent with the tribe’s position at the crossroads of the north-south and east-west routes through Canaan — a strategic location requiring substantial defensive capacity.
6: The three-son list for Benjamin (Bela, Becher, Jediael) represents a different Benjaminite tribal tradition from chapter 8’s extended list. Benjamin occupied the narrow highland strip between Jerusalem and the Jordan valley, a strategically critical corridor that controlled access to the ford at Jericho. The tribe’s position on the border between Judah and Ephraim gave it significance both in the united monarchy (Saul) and in the divided kingdom (Benjamin straddled the line between northern and southern territories).
7: Bela’s five sons and the 22,034-warrior count situate this genealogy in the administrative apparatus of the Davidic kingdom. Archaeological surveys of the Benjaminite highlands show relatively dense settlement patterns in the Iron Age I–II period, consistent with a substantial tribal population capable of fielding this size force. The term “mighty warriors” (gibbôrê ḥayil) is used in administrative texts from this period to designate professional soldiers or military reservists distinguishable from levied farmers.
8: Becher’s nine sons include Anathoth, the place-name later associated with Jeremiah’s priestly family (Jeremiah 1:1). Anathoth (modern Ras el-Kharrubeh, about 3 miles northeast of Jerusalem) was a Benjaminite Levitical city (Joshua 21:18). The embedding of place-names as personal names in genealogies is a common West Semitic practice; it records which family first settled and controlled a particular site. Anathoth’s appearance here as Becher’s son establishes the Benjaminite genealogical claim to that locality.
9: The 20,200 warriors enrolled for Becher’s line, alongside the technical term for genealogical registration, reflects the administrative reality of the post-exilic period: families returning from Babylonian exile needed documented tribal affiliation to claim land, temple participation rights, and community standing. The Chronicler’s preservation of these enrollment figures served a living community function for the returnees, not merely historical interest.
10: Jediael’s son Bilhan produces seven military sub-commanders. The name Bilhan appears also in an Edomite context (Genesis 36:27), reflecting the naming overlap between early Israelite and Edomite tribal groups along the southern corridor. The seven-son list follows the common West Semitic pattern of organizing hereditary offices into a symmetrical numerical group, representing the full complement of military leadership for a major clan.
11: Jediael’s 17,200 warriors “fit for military service” is the smallest of the three Benjaminite sub-lists in 1 Chronicles 7, but the formal conscription language (“those fit for going out to battle”) indicates this was a real administrative classification. In the ancient Near East, military fitness assessments distinguished between those able to serve as active combatants and those assigned to logistics or garrison duty; this enrollment category appears to cover the active combat pool.
12: The cryptic verse about Shuppim, Huppim, and Hushim son of Aher has long been recognized as a Dan fragment. Dan’s son is named Hushim in Genesis 46:23 (also called Shuham in Numbers 26:42). The near-total omission of Dan from 1 Chronicles’ genealogies (1 Chronicles 2–9) is striking. Some scholars connect it to Dan’s association with illegitimate cult centers (Judg 18; 1 Kings 12:29–30); others see it as a coincidence of damaged source material. The same silence about Dan appears in Revelation 7:4–8, where Dan is omitted from the 144,000. The deliberate or incidental suppression of Dan’s genealogy reflects the tribe’s complex and controversial history.
13: Naphtali’s four-name list (with the single note “from Bilhah”) receives minimal treatment compared to the extended genealogies of Issachar, Benjamin, and Ephraim. Naphtali occupied the northern Galilee highlands and the western shore of the Sea of Galilee. The region was heavily Canaanite and later Aramaean in cultural influence; Isaiah’s “Galilee of the nations” (Isaiah 9:1) reflects this mixed character. Naphtali’s role in the Deborah coalition was significant (Judges 4:6; 1 Chronicles 5:18), but the tribe receives less Chronicler attention than the southern and central tribes whose descendants formed the core of the post-exilic community.
14: Manasseh’s Transjordanian territory (Gilead) through Machir reflects the historical reality of Israelite settlement east of the Jordan. Gilead was a contested border zone: the region passed between Israelite, Moabite, Ammonite, and Aramaean control across the centuries. The Aramean concubine in Machir’s household reflects the intermarriage pattern documented in the Tell Dan stele and other Aramaean inscriptions, where the boundaries between Israelite and Aramaean populations in this region were porous.
15: The reference to Zelophehad (“who had only daughters”) connects this genealogy to one of the most significant legal precedents in the Pentateuch. The daughters of Zelophehad (Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, Tirzah) petitioned Moses for the right to inherit their father’s portion when he died without male heirs (Numbers 27:1–11). YHWH’s ruling in their favour established a permanent inheritance statute (Numbers 27:8–11) that allowed daughters to inherit when there were no sons. The genealogical note here grounds that legal case in the specific family history of Manassite Machir’s line.
16: Maachah as Machir’s wife (the same name as the Aramean kingdom north of Dan) confirms the cultural overlap between northern Manasseh and Aramean groups. The kingdom of Maachah (2 Samuel 10:6, 8) was one of several small Aramaean polities in the Transjordan-Hauran region whose relationship with Israel alternated between alliance and conflict. The personal name Maachah crossing ethnic boundaries mirrors the historical reality of intermarriage along these borders.
17: The Bedan mentioned here as a descendant of Gilead appears in 1 Samuel 12:11 in Samuel’s historical review as one of the judges sent to deliver Israel. Some manuscripts read “Barak” in 1 Samuel 12:11 instead of Bedan; the variation may reflect a judge from Manassite Gilead who was less prominent in the Ephraimite-centered judge traditions but preserved in the Transjordanian genealogical memory. Gilead produced several distinctive leaders (Jephthah, Elijah the Tishbite) who operated at the margins of the main Cisjordanian tribal narrative.
18: Hammoleketh (“the queen”) is one of several women in 1 Chronicles’ genealogies who appear as founders of lineages or places. Her descendants include Abiezer, the ancestor of Gideon (Judges 6:11). The Abiezrite clan whose land included the great oak of Ophrah was situated in the western portion of Manasseh. The association of a woman-founder with Gideon’s clan may preserve a pre-patriarchal memory of female leadership in the Manassite highlands, consistent with matrilineal inheritance traditions in some adjacent cultures.
19: Shemida and his four sons are attested in the Samaria Ostraca (8th century BCE administrative pottery sherds from the city of Samaria), where Shemida appears as a clan name associated with wine-production districts. The Samaria Ostraca are among the most important epigraphic finds from the northern kingdom and demonstrate that the clan names in Numbers 26 and 1 Chronicles reflect genuine administrative units still functional centuries after the initial settlement period.
20: Ephraim’s linear genealogy through Shuthelah begins the longest tribal section in 1 Chronicles 7 (vv20–29). Ephraim occupied the central highland ridge between the Jezreel valley and the Shephelah — the strategic heart of the northern kingdom. The highlands of Ephraim (modern West Bank hill country around Nablus and Ramallah) were well-forested and defensible; the tribe’s central position made it dominant in the pre-monarchic period and the tribal nucleus of the northern kingdom after 931 BCE.
21: The Ephraimite cattle raid on Gath reflects the historical proximity of Ephraimite settlers to Philistine territories during the early Iron Age. Gath (Tell es-Safi) was the largest Philistine city and lay at the edge of the Shephelah, accessible from the Ephraimite highlands through the Vale of Elah. Archaeological excavations at Gath have revealed destruction layers consistent with repeated conflicts with neighbouring populations in the Iron Age I period (12th–10th centuries BCE), the era when this episode likely occurred.
22: Ephraim’s extended mourning for his sons killed in the Gath raid places the patriarch in a position analogous to Jacob grieving for Joseph. The grief narrative embedded in a genealogy is a literary technique for humanizing an otherwise administrative register. Ancient Near Eastern funerary practices included extended mourning periods; Egyptian and Ugaritic texts describe multi-day mourning rituals for fallen warriors. The brothers’ consolation visit reflects the kinship-solidarity system that organized Israelite clan life.
23: The birth of Beriah as consolation after loss is a pattern in biblical genealogy: Isaac born to Abraham and Sarah after years of barrenness; Benjamin born to Jacob after Rachel’s death; Samuel born to Hannah after her humiliation. The Ephraimite Beriah tradition may have been preserved because Beriah’s line became important in the later Ephraimite community — his name, encoding the memory of family tragedy, served as a constant reminder of the tribe’s founding grief.
25: The linear descent from Rephah and Resheph toward Joshua spans approximately six to eight generations, placing the genealogy in the pre-conquest period. The transition from the personal-narrative elements (vv21–24) back to the linear descent sequence signals the return to official tribal documentation. These names (Rephah, Resheph, Tahan) are otherwise unknown but function to bridge the genealogy between the Beriah story and the theologically crucial name of Joshua at v27.
26: Ladan and Ammihud are intermediate ancestors between the Ephraimite clans and Elishama, who appears in Numbers as the tribal prince of Ephraim (Numbers 1:10; 1 Chronicles 2:18; 1 Chronicles 7:48–53). Elishama led the Ephraimite division of the wilderness camp, marshalled for the tribal march, and offered the Ephraimite dedication gifts for the tabernacle. His position in this genealogy confirms the Chronicler’s use of a source that connected the Numbers tribal leaders to the extended Ephraimite family tree.
27: Joshua son of Nun (here: Non) was not only Israel’s military leader in the conquest but the first leader to bear a name formed with the YHWH prefix (Numbers 13:16; Moses renamed Hoshea to Yehoshua). His Ephraimite lineage is historically significant: the tribal affiliation of Israel’s military saviour in the conquest was the same tribe that would later become the dominant tribe of the northern kingdom. The Joshua genealogy grounds the conquest narrative in a specific Ephraimite family, not a mythologized hero figure.
28: The Ephraimite territorial catalogue (Bethel, Naaran, Gezer, Shechem, Ayyah) preserves the pre-Solomonic Ephraimite land-claims. Shechem was Israel’s first capital under Jeroboam (1 Kings 12:25) and a major cultic and administrative centre before Jerusalem; its inclusion in the Ephraimite territorial list confirms the tribe’s attachment to this ancient covenant site (Gen 34; Josh 24). Gezer, at the foothills of the Shephelah, was the last major Canaanite city-state to fall to Israelite control, eventually given to Solomon as Egyptian royal dowry (1 Kings 9:16–17).
29: Manassite border cities along the Jezreel corridor (Beth-shean, Taanach, Megiddo, Dor) were the most heavily fortified urban centres in Canaan during the Late Bronze Age. Egyptian pharaohs used this corridor as the primary route for military campaigns into Syria; the Battle of Megiddo (ca. 1457 BCE under Thutmose III) took place at this location. The Israelite failure to displace these Canaanite city-states (Judges 1:27–28) was a persistent military weakness; the cities eventually came under Israelite control during the Solomonic period through administrative reorganization rather than conquest (1 Kings 9:15–19).
30: Asher’s coastal territory stretched from the Carmel promontory northward through the Akko plain and into the region of Tyre and Sidon. The tribe was positioned along one of the most commercially active coastlines in the ancient world: Phoenician cities (Tyre, Sidon, Byblos) were Asher’s northern neighbours, and Asher’s genealogy reflects the cultural contact between Israelite and Phoenician populations. The failure to displace the coastal cities (Judges 1:31–32) meant Asher remained in a semi-integrated relationship with Canaanite coastal culture throughout the Judges period.
31: Beriah and Malchiel of Asher’s tribe appear in Genesis 46:17 and Numbers 26:44–45; the genealogy here extends their lines. Malchiel’s son Birzaith (from a root possibly meaning “olive/olive-related”) points to Asher’s olive-oil economy: the coastal slopes of the Carmel range and the Galilee hills were among the most productive olive-growing zones in ancient Palestine. Asher is blessed in Deuteronomy 33:24 with oil from rock (“let him dip his foot in oil”), reflecting the tribe’s agricultural specialization.
32: Heber’s three sons and sister Shua illustrate the bilateral kinship system that preserved female names alongside male in certain genealogical traditions. A non-Israelite Heber of the same name appears in Judges 4:11–22 (Jael’s husband, a Kenite), reflecting the name’s broader West Semitic distribution. The Asherite Heber’s line receives the most extended genealogical treatment in the chapter (vv32–39), indicating this sub-clan retained historical prominence in the traditions the Chronicler compiled.
33: Japhlet’s three sons and the territorial association with Joshua 16:3 suggest the Japhletite clan held land on the Ephraimite-Asherite border. The Japhlet’s border in Joshua is described as the boundary between Ephraim’s allotment and the descent toward the coast — positioning this clan as controllers of the transition zone between highland agriculture and coastal commerce. Such border clans often had mixed ethnic and economic profiles in the ancient Near East.
34: The sub-names within Heber’s genealogy (Rohgah, Jehubbah, Aram) include Aram as a personal name, consistent with Asher’s northern position near Aramean-influenced territory. The Aramaean cultural sphere extended well into northern Galilee; personal names reflecting Aramean cultural contact are unsurprising in an Asherite context. The name Aram in an Israelite tribal genealogy does not indicate Aramean ethnicity but rather the cultural borrowing common in border zones.
35: The identification of Helem with Hotham (v32) is textually plausible: the Hebrew letters of Hotham and Helem are sufficiently similar to account for scribal variation (ḥôṯām / ḥêlem). Scribal confusion between similar letter-forms is a recognized source of textual variation in Hebrew manuscripts; the Masoretic Text preserves the variant spellings without harmonizing them. Zophah heads the next sub-list, providing the next level of Asherite descent.
36: Zophah’s six sons include names with non-Semitic or mixed linguistic backgrounds (Harnepher, Shual), reflecting the cultural mixing in Asher’s coastal zone. Archaeological evidence from the Akko plain (Asher’s heartland) shows substantial Egyptian administrative presence in the Late Bronze Age and Sea People/Phoenician cultural influence in Iron Age I. Israelite tribal populations in this region would have interacted with a diverse cultural matrix not reflected in the more homogenous central highland groups.
37: Jether’s sons and Ulla’s sons close the Asherite sub-lists. The 26,000-warrior total for Asher is the smallest in 1 Chronicles 7, reflecting either demographic reality (a smaller Asherite population) or the tribe’s maritime/commercial rather than military orientation. Asher’s position on the coast meant that military service was more relevant for defence against coastal raids than for the highland tribal conflicts that shaped the warrior culture of Issachar, Benjamin, and Ephraim.
38: Jether’s three sons appear without further comment. Jephunneh as an Asherite name (also the name of Caleb’s father) illustrates the circulation of prestige names across tribal boundaries. Caleb’s father Jephunneh was a Kenizzite (non-Israelite), and his son Caleb was nonetheless a Judahite hero; names did not mark fixed ethnic or tribal identity but could cross those lines through adoption, marriage, or migration.
39: Ulla’s three sons (Arah, Hanniel, Rizia) close the Asherite genealogy. Arah appears in the post-exilic returnee list (Ezra 2:5; Nehemiah 7:10) as a family with 775 members, confirming that Asherite clan names survived into the Babylonian exile community and returned under Zerubbabel. The Chronicler’s preservation of Asherite genealogical detail served this returning community’s need to verify tribal credentials for land reallocation in the Persian period.
40: The closing formula for Asher (“heads of ancestral houses, chosen men, mighty warriors, outstanding leaders; 26,000 enrolled for military service”) is the most elaborated of the 1 Chronicles 7 tribal summaries. The elevated language compensates for the tribe’s small warrior count by emphasizing the quality of its leadership. Post-exilic Persian province administration required documented tribal affiliations for tax assessment, land allocation, and temple-service duty; the genealogical records the Chronicler compiled served as the constitutional foundation for the restored community.
